body:
## Summary
Make `JLD2Writer(overwrite_existing=true)` remove an existing output file when the writer is initialized, rather than deleting files later based on per-iteration collisions.

## Changes
- move `overwrite_existing` handling to `initialize!`
- stop deleting and reinitializing JLD2 files inside `write_output!`
- update the `overwrite_existing` docstring to match the new behavior

closes #5643
title: Make JLD2 `overwrite_existing` file-based

body:
Addresses #5651.

## Problem

`heatmap!(ax, f)` draws on a rectangular mesh that ignores the grid geometry, so a vertical slice of a terrain-following field — or a panel of an `OrthogonalSphericalShellGrid` — shows flat layers and the curvature is invisible (the plotting path goes through the 1-D `znodes`/`rnodes` vector). Makie's `heatmap` only takes 1-D `x, y`, so the fix is a mesh-based path.

## What this adds

`quadmesh!` — a `pcolormesh`-style plot where each cell is drawn as a quadrilateral from its four corner coordinates and filled with a single flat color. Fields on curvilinear meshes render in their true geometry; unlike `surface!` the color is flat per cell rather than Gouraud-interpolated.

**Pass a `Field` — corners are derived from its grid (no coordinate bookkeeping):**

```julia
using Oceananigans, CairoMakie

quadmesh!(ax, f)          # 2-D Field → flat-shaded slice in its true coordinates
fig, ax, plt = quadmesh(f)
```

- A horizontal field on a `LatitudeLongitudeGrid` / `OrthogonalSphericalShellGrid` is drawn as a **3-D Cartesian shell** (use an `Axis3`).
- Any other 2-D field (one reduced or `Flat` dimension) is a **2-D slice** in its two active coordinates; the vertical is built from the scalar `znode`, so **terrain-following coordinates keep their true curvature**.

**Or pass corner arrays directly** for full control (e.g. composing several slices into a 3-D box):

```julia
quadmesh!(ax,  xc, yc, vals)       # corners (P+1, Q+1), values (P, Q)
quadmesh!(ax3, xc, yc, zc, vals)   # 3-D panel on an Axis3
```

**Visualize the grid itself** — `quadmesh!(ax, grid)` draws the cells as a wireframe (edges, no fill), from the same corners:

```julia
quadmesh!(ax3, grid)   # spherical grid → 3-D shell graticule (lat-lon / tripolar / cubed-sphere)
quadmesh!(ax,  grid)   # grid with one Flat dimension → 2-D wireframe
```

`vals` may be an `Observable` (geometry built once, only color updates — for animations); `drop_nan_cells` omits NaN cells; returns the `Mesh` plot so `Colorbar(fig[…], plt)` works; `colormap`/`colorrange`/`nan_color`/`alpha` pass through.

**Implementation.** 4 duplicated vertices + 2 triangles per quad, with the cell value repeated across its 4 vertices and passed as the `color` kwarg — equal corner colors degenerate Gouraud shading to a flat fill, which renders identically across backends (CairoMakie has no per-face color path). The `quadmesh`/`quadmesh!` stubs are declared in the main package and methods added in `OceananigansMakieExt`, so `using Oceananigans` (+ a backend) surfaces them.

## Verified

Headless tests in `test/test_makie_ext.jl` (coordinate-array 2-D/3-D, the `Field` method on rectilinear x–z and spherical grids, the 3-D-field error, color-buffer length, `Colorbar`, NaN dropping, the `Observable` path) — **20/20 pass**. Used to render: a terrain-following x–z slice of a compressible quasi-LES in physical coordinates (mesh boundary coincides with the analytic terrain `h(x)`), a `LatitudeLongitudeGrid` shell, and a `TripolarGrid` (the displaced-pole seam is visible) — all via `quadmesh!(ax, f)`.

## Follow-ups (per #5651)

`FieldTimeSeries` method (reactive color), a `@recipe`, the `heatmap!`-auto-promote decision, `contourf` on a mesh, and coordinating the `OrthogonalSphericalShellGrid` overlap with Imaginocean.jl.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: Add `quadmesh!` for flat-shaded curvilinear-grid plotting
